_note: Glass Box demo GALLERY (RAILS MODE replay). Preselected questions grouped by business outcome (Grow Revenue / See the Real Risk / Stop the Leakage / Operate with Confidence). Each question is a presenter-paced STORY: plain-English beats revealed one click at a time, the 'aha' beat flagged link:true, ending in a plain answer. Beats may carry a `record` (the raw row, shown in the audit drawer). This is an authored replay, NOT live engine output. The live contextOS engine replaces this fixture at backend/api/routes/demo.py (same beat contract); no silent fallback.

questions.question: What's the real run-rate for Engineering?

beats.text: Two systems disagree on Engineering's run-rate.
Workday — your system of record — says $2.64M.
A shadow CRM claims $8.64M — but that record is cancelled, from an unauthorized source.
So the $8.64M is dropped before the LLM ever sees it.

answer.headline: $2.64M
answer.sub: the trustworthy number — the conflicting $8.64M was pruned, not averaged in

questions.question: Which of our client's suppliers should we be banking?

beats.text: You bank Vantage Foods. They move $540M a year.
$92M of it goes to one supplier — Cedar Logistics.
Cedar banks somewhere else — and has never been your customer.
Six more companies like Cedar sit in Vantage's payment network.

answer.headline: $6.2M / yr
answer.sub: in new business — every prospect reachable through a client you already have

questions.question: What's our real exposure to Brightwater Partners?

beats.text: Two trading desks each face Brightwater Partners.
Prime brokerage holds $320M. Financing holds another $420M.
They're booked under two different legal entities — that roll up to the same parent. Nobody had added them together.

record.children: Brightwater Markets Ltd, Brightwater Capital LLC

beats.text: After netting, your true exposure is far bigger than either desk saw.

answer.headline: $612M net
answer.sub: the real number — not the $320M or $420M any one desk was watching

beats.text: The Whitfield household keeps $3.2M with you.
But money keeps arriving from an outside broker.
Those transfers trace to a $7.8M account held away — money you don't manage.
It's a clean fit for a managed-portfolio and trust mandate.

answer.headline: $7.8M held away
answer.sub: ≈ $58K/yr in fees to win back — surfaced by the money flow, not any statement

questions.question: What's our real hurricane exposure on the Gulf Coast?

beats.text: 4,210 policies sit in the Gulf Coast wind zone — $1.9B insured.
All correctly tagged to the hurricane peril.
But a $280M commercial book in the same county was never tagged. Hidden accumulation.
After reinsurance, the retained loss is what actually hits you.

answer.headline: $780M net retained
answer.sub: including $280M the catastrophe model never saw

questions.question: Which discharges are most likely to bounce back?

beats.text: 142 heart-failure patients were just discharged.
Many already aren't refilling their medication.
Their follow-up was never booked — the referral went out-of-network and got denied. A clinical risk created by an admin gap.
A transport barrier compounds it.

answer.headline: 31 patients
answer.sub: in the readmission top decile — and you can see exactly why

questions.question: Where are referrals leaking out of network?

beats.text: 312 cardiology referrals went to one specialist last quarter.
Your provider directory says she's in-network.
But the claims prove she's out-of-network for your biggest plan. The directory is stale.
Three in-network peers could absorb those referrals.

questions.question: Why do our claims keep getting denied?

beats.text: Most denials come back as 'missing prior authorization.'
They look like seven different providers each making mistakes.
But all seven use one order template that turns the auth flag off. One template, not seven people.

answer.headline: $640K
answer.sub: in denials — traced to a single order template you can fix in an afternoon

questions.question: Are any patients on dangerous medication combinations?

beats.text: A cardiologist prescribes an anticoagulant.
A primary-care doctor adds a high-dose anti-inflammatory.
Neither saw the other's chart. Together, it's a major bleeding risk.
Filled at different pharmacies, so no single med list ever caught it.

answer.headline: 218 patients
answer.sub: on a dangerous combination no single chart would have shown

questions.question: If Salesforce goes down, what actually breaks?

beats.text: Salesforce feeds 14 live integrations.
One of them drives your quote-to-cash service.
And the month-end financial close quietly depends on a nightly Salesforce export — with no runbook and no on-call owner.

answer.headline: 9 systems · 2 teams
answer.sub: in the blast radius — including the close nobody had mapped
